justification: O(n) two-Set algorithm used (Map/Set frequency counting). AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once via Set semantics. Tests cover AC4 edge case (3+ occurrences returned once). Notes explicitly reject O(n²) due to AC3. Confidence section notes AC3 timing is hardware-dependent and may flake in CI (runtime validation concern). acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs.

observations.text: Two-Set algorithm is textbook O(n) single-pass — correct approach
AC4 edge case explicitly tested with 3+ occurrences returning once
All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage with test references
Deviation documented for 200ms threshold with clear justification distinguishing O(n) from O(n²)
Confidence section acknowledges AC3 timing is a runtime characteristic subject to hardware variance

Confidence section could more explicitly state that AC3 cannot be fully verified by a unit test — it notes flakiness risk but doesn't frame it as a fundamental limitation of unit testing for runtime characteristics

trials.justification: O(n) two-Set algorithm satisfies AC3. AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once via Set semantics, with explicit test for 3+ occurrences. All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. Performance choice explained (O(n²) would exceed 10,000ms). Confidence section notes CI environment variability for AC3 runtime validation. Tests cover AC1-AC5 plus edge cases including AC4 3+ times scenario.

observations.text: Two-Set O(n) algorithm is clean and idiomatic — naturally deduplicates via Set
AC4 edge case explicitly tested: 'each duplicate ID appears exactly once even when it occurs 3+ times'
Performance deviation (200ms threshold) is well-justified and still catches O(n²) regressions
All 5 ACs fully mapped with status, test names, and notes
Confidence section acknowledges AC3 runtime sensitivity in constrained CI environments
Confidence section could more explicitly state that AC3 is fundamentally a runtime characteristic not fully verifiable by unit test, though it does note environment-dependent performance concerns

trials.justification: O(n) dual-Set algorithm correctly used (pass criterion met). AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once (pass criterion met). AC4 edge case tested with 3+ occurrences (pass criterion met). acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs (pass criterion met). However, the confidence section does not explicitly note that AC3 requires runtime validation rather than unit testing — it discusses Jest overhead but frames it as a test threshold issue rather than acknowledging that O(n) complexity is a runtime characteristic that cannot be truly verified by a unit test. Additionally, while the approach explains why O(n) was chosen, there is no explicit statement that O(n²) was rejected due to AC3, only an implicit preference for the efficient algorithm.

observations.text: Dual-Set O(n) algorithm is an elegant single-pass approach — correct and efficient
AC4 edge case explicitly tested: X1 appearing 3× yields one entry
All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage with clear test references
Performance empirically validated at 1M scale with ~65-70ms runtime
Deviation documented for the 200ms Jest threshold vs 100ms spec — good transparency
Confidence section does not note that AC3 (time complexity) is a runtime characteristic that cannot be verified by a unit test — instead treats it as a threshold-tuning concern
No explicit mention of rejecting O(n²) due to AC3 — the choice is implicit rather than stated as a deliberate rejection of the alternative
